title: Autumn simmer pot
subtitle: A warm spiced apple simmerpot with cinnamon, cloves, star anise, and orange peel
excerpt: A simmer pot is the simplest form of natural home fragrance: aromatic ingredients simmered in water on the lowest possible heat. The steam carries the scent through the kitchen and beyond. Top up the water every 30 minutes and never let it boil dry.
sourceNotes: Simmer pot tradition documented across North American, British, and European homesteading literature.

recipe.yieldDescription: One pot of autumn fragrance (lasts 2 to 4 hours per session)
recipe.batchNotes: Ingredients are used per session. Pre-pack batches of dried ingredients in jars or bags for convenience.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Ingredients can be combined dry and stored for months. Add water and simmer fresh each time.
recipe.temperatureNote: Simmer on the lowest possible heat. The water should barely tremble at the surface, not boil.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Dried ingredients keep for 12 months. Fresh apple slices should be used the same day. The simmered liquid can be refrigerated and reused once; discard after the second use.

recipeTools.notes: Dedicated to simmer pot use; the spice residue is hard to remove

glossaryTerms.definition: A saucepan of water containing aromatic ingredients simmered on the lowest heat. The steam releases volatile fragrant compounds into the air. Unlike candles or sprays, a simmer pot produces a humid, natural fragrance that fills the kitchen quickly.

Fragrant molecules that evaporate readily at low temperatures, entering the air as a gas and triggering the sense of smell. Cinnamon, clove, and citrus peel are rich in volatile aromatic compounds that are released slowly by simmering.
The seed pod of Illicium verum, with a warm, sweet anise scent. Used whole in simmer pots for its aromatic contribution. Distinct from common anise seed though similarly flavoured.

in 1 litre of water. Keep the heat at its absolute lowest; boiling drives off the fragrance faster and is wasteful. Top up with water every 30 minutes and never let the pot boil dry. The simmered liquid can be refrigerated and used once more before discarding.

items.prepNote: Fresh gives a brighter, fruitier scent; dried is more intense and earthy
Added to the pot at the start

content.text: Combine all ingredients in the saucepan with 1 litre of water. Bring to the gentlest possible simmer over the lowest heat setting. Once simmering, there is no further work required: simply keep an eye on the water level. Top up with hot water every 30 minutes. The fragrance builds over the first 20 minutes and continues for 2 to 4 hours.

Dried ingredients pre-mixed without water keep for 12 months in a sealed jar. The simmered liquid can be cooled, covered, and refrigerated for one more use. Discard after 48 hours or at the second use, whichever comes first. Never leave the pot unattended on the hob.
